summary: My family moved to Jacksonville, Florida, from Flint, Michigan, in 1870 when I was very young. We lived in South Jacksonville near the St. Johns River, boarding first with ex-Governor Harrison Reed's family. Jacksonville grew rapidly during my youth, and I witnessed events like the Great Fire of 1901 from across the river. I also attended school here and watched the city develop with the coming of the Florida East Coast Railway and the building of bridges connecting Jacksonville and South Jacksonville.

I was born in Flint, Michigan, but my family moved to Florida when I was a small child in 1870 because my father was ill with consumption. The Gibsons were early settlers in Michigan, and my father was of the fourth generation there. Though we left Flint behind, our roots and family history remained important to us as we settled in Jacksonville.

Harrison Reed was the first Territorial Governor of Florida and a Yankee from Milwaukee, Wisconsin, like my family. When we arrived in Jacksonville, we boarded with his family in his old Reed house on Platt Street in South Jacksonville. His son, Harrison Reed Jr., was my close playmate during childhood. Governor Reed was also the brother of Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, linking two prominent families in the area.

Milwaukee, Wisconsin, was the original home of Governor Harrison Reed and Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, who were siblings. My family, also Yankees, found a connection with them when we moved to Florida. The Mitchell family, including Mrs. Alexander Mitchell and her son David, had ties back to Milwaukee, which was often mentioned in family stories and court proceedings.

My father began working at the Barnett Bank as a bookkeeper and was the third man employed there. The bank was then called the Bank of Jacksonville and was located at the corner of Forsyth and Main, now Duval Street. By the time of my father's death in 1891, he was assistant cashier. I have seen the bank grow from a small operation with three employees to a large institution employing hundreds.

Forsyth Street was the location of the Barnett Bank when my father worked there in the late 1800s. The bank stood at the corner of Forsyth and Main, which is now known as Duval Street. This area was central to Jacksonville's business district as it grew during that period.

Duval Street, formerly known as Main Street, was part of the downtown Jacksonville area where the Barnett Bank was located. My father worked at the bank on the southwest corner of Forsyth and Main, which today is called Duval Street. This street was a hub of commerce as Jacksonville expanded.

Mrs. Alexander Mitchell was the sister of Governor Harrison Reed and a prominent figure in Jacksonville society. She owned Villa Alexandria on A. Johns Avenue and was a cultured, dignified woman who traveled widely. I often stayed with her as a child and admired her beautiful home and refined manners. Her son, John Mitchell, had a son named David, who figures prominently in our family stories.

Villa Alexandria was the grand home of Mrs. Alexander Mitchell on A. Johns Avenue in north Jacksonville. It was a large, three-story frame house with exquisitely decorated rooms, including a French room and a Japanese bedroom. I often stayed there as a child and was fascinated by the beautiful furnishings, imported china, and the magnificent dining room overlooking the St. Johns River.

The St. Johns River was a constant presence in my life growing up in Jacksonville. Our property was fenced to the river's edge, and I remember crossing it by rowboat daily, especially when visiting friends or going to school. The river also provided a vantage point to witness events like the Great Fire of 1901 and was the site of early ferryboats such as the Armington and the Mechanic.

David Mitchell was the young son of John Mitchell and grandson of Mrs. Alexander Mitchell. I first saw him in 1893 when his grandmother brought him back to South Jacksonville after a family divorce. He was a lively boy, fond of his bulldogs, and was the apple of his grandmother's eye. Later, he married Kittie Parrott, and they had a son named Alexander Mitchell. David suffered a serious polo accident that left him incapacitated for a long time.

Kittie Parrott, originally Kittie Sutton, was the woman who married David Mitchell. She was adopted by Mr. Parrott after her mother remarried and was determined to marry the richest man in Jacksonville, setting her sights on David. Their marriage produced a son, Alexander Mitchell, but after David's accident, Kittie sought a divorce and later caused scandal by living with another man before eventually remarrying and settling in West Palm Beach.

The Florida East Coast Railway played a significant role in Jacksonville's development during my youth. The Mechanic ferryboat, owned by the Flagler system, carried passengers and freight across the river to the railway station. Kittie Parrott's father was an important official with the railway, which added to the family's prominence. The railway's expansion coincided with the building of bridges that eventually joined Jacksonville and South Jacksonville.

West Palm Beach, Florida, became the later home of Kittie Parrott after her tumultuous years in Jacksonville. Following her divorce from David Mitchell and a scandalous relationship, she eventually remarried and settled in West Palm Beach. It was there she regained some respectability after her earlier wild behavior.

The Armington was the first ferryboat operating on the St. Johns River around 1894 or 1896. I used to take this ferry to cross over to Jacksonville to attend the Bradford Institute, a private Episcopal school. It was a small but important means of transportation before the bridge was built connecting Jacksonville and South Jacksonville.

The Bradford Institute was a private school in Jacksonville operated by Mrs. Drow Williams under the Episcopal Church. I attended this school and often crossed the St. Johns River on the Armington ferryboat to get there. It was a notable part of my early education and social life in the city.

The Episcopal Church was connected to the Bradford Institute, the private school I attended in Jacksonville. Mrs. Drow Williams operated the school under its auspices, providing a religious and educational foundation during my youth. The church's influence was part of the community fabric at that time.

The Mechanic was the second ferryboat on the St. Johns River, owned by the Florida East Coast Railway's Flagler system. It transported freight and passengers between the foot of Hogan Street and the south side railway station. This ferry was part of the growing transportation network before the bridge was completed.

The Florida East Coast Railway was instrumental in Jacksonville's growth, operating ferryboats like the Mechanic and expanding rail service. My family had connections through Kittie Parrott's father, an important official with the railway. The railway's development coincided with infrastructure improvements, including the bridge joining Jacksonville and South Jacksonville.

I vividly remember the Great Fire of 1901 in Jacksonville, which started around noon on May 3rd. My siblings and I watched the flames and smoke from the south side of the St. Johns River, climbing onto our roof for a better view. The fire destroyed much of the city, including the Catholic Church, and left many homeless. My father stayed at the Barnett Bank through the night to keep things running, and our home sheltered nineteen displaced guests.

The Women's Club of Jacksonville was located in a block that was destroyed during the Great Fire of 1901. I recall that the fire consumed everything in that area, emphasizing the devastation the city faced. The club's site marked part of the downtown that had to be rebuilt after the disaster.

Harrison Reed Jr., the son of Governor Reed, worked for the Lite Hubbard Hardware Co. after finishing school. Later, he moved on to a position with the Standard Oil Company, eventually becoming Florida manager. His early employment at Lite Hubbard was the start of his career in Jacksonville's business community.

Standard Oil was a major company in Jacksonville where Harrison Reed Jr. worked after leaving Lite Hubbard Hardware Co. He was initially in charge of their warehouse and later promoted to Florida manager. The company grew in influence during his tenure, reflecting the economic development of the city.

San Jose is a north Jacksonville suburb near where Mrs. Alexander Mitchell's Villa Alexandria was located on A. Johns Avenue. This area was fashionable and home to prominent families like the Mitchells. My family had connections here through visits and property ownership.

While not directly mentioned in detail, All Saints Chapel was part of the Episcopal Church community connected to the Bradford Institute where I studied. The chapel represented the religious life in Jacksonville during my youth and was part of the church's presence in the city.

The yellow fever epidemic of 1888 is not directly mentioned in my recollections, but it was a significant event in Florida's history during my childhood years. My family had moved to Jacksonville by then, and such epidemics shaped public health and community responses in the region.
